titleStmt.title: Letter from Terence Duffin to his father Adam, 29 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Terrence Duffin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: The letter was sent by Major Terence Duffin (1886 - 1936), to his father Adam Duffin (1841 – 1924). In the letter Terence writes that the weather has been making life in the trenches much easier, although he separates himself from the men as he acknowledges that he is living in ‘comparative luxury’. Duffin reports that they have just had a visit from Bishop Crozier (1853 – 1920), the primate, and Sir James Stronge (1849 – 1928). According to Duffin, their guests were fed with ‘caviar and game and oysters’ for dinner leaving them pleased with warfare feeding. However, it is not all comfortable living as Duffin writes about his fears about the frequent number of gas attacks reported in the last few days in their vicinity. However, Duffin goes on to state that the reports have so far come to nothing and instead tells his father of his riding exercises and a pony which has gone lame.This letter is part of a rich correspondence between various members of the Duffin family, a large prosperous family living in Strandtown, Belfast. Several family members served in the war, including Major Terence Duffin, who served as a staff officer with 107 Brigade, and later with Royal Irish Rifles; Major Charles G Duffin MC, Royal Field Artillery; and their sisters Emma, Celia and Dorothy who served as Voluntary Aid Detachments (VADS) in Egypt and France, and with the YMCA.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Dear Father, We have had quite a
longish spell of dry weather and
consequently have been getting through
much more work in the trenches
and generally more comfortable
all round â When I say
'we' I don't include myself as
I am still living in comparative
luxury. We are on the
move again â our original
Division is going to take our
place and we still remain on
with our present Divisn but
go back for a rest, somewhere. We don't yet know for how
long or exactly where but anyhow
out if the danger zone â Naturally we are all glad
that we are staying on with
the present Division but rather
surprised, as we were told only
3 days ago that we were to
go back to the original one
which would have been a
very unpopular move for all
ranks. We have just had a visit
from Bishop Crozier (The Primate)
and Sir James Stronge â The
Primate was in great form
and really quite amusing
Sir Jas. Stronge, a stupid old
goat.They were fearfully
pleased at being allowed
to go round here â We did
them proud â We had
caviar and game and
oysters for dinner, which &
sent him away quite pleased
with warfare feeding â Day, who is a distant relation
of the Primates, took them up
some of the communicating
trenches and they were able
to visit a battalion Hd. Qrs. Even then they got 2 shells
uncomfortably near! The
Primate should have found
subjects for sermons for months
to come! I am sending you some
post cards of a shell battered
village quite close where and
which has become quite famous
on account of the Church, which
has been fairly well smashed
up but the spire is still standing
with an enormous figure
of the Virgin hanging down at
an extraordinary angle and
apparently without support. We took the Primate to see
it on his last day. We have been much perturbed
during the last few days by
frequent reports of gas attacks
in the vicinity and warnings to be
prepared but so far they have
all come to nothing. Very little else to tell you
at present - I keep in
first rate form and have
snared one or two afternoons
for riding exercise lately as
work has not been quite so
heavy â One of my ponies
was lame but has now
recovered and mighty
fresh today when I had
been out for the first time â I am some what stiff as
a result. The supernumerary Staff
Captain is lost to us again â
The officer from the 15 th
who took over when I was
on leave was re-called as
the Adjutant had been
wounded and so far
we have got no one in
his place. Best love to all
from your own Terry
